questions.q: What is precision in the context of classification metrics, and how does it differ from recall?
options: Precision and recall are identical metrics with different names
Precision measures the fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive; recall measures the fraction of actual positives that the model correctly identified
Precision only applies to binary classification tasks
Recall is more important than precision for all NLP tasks
questions.explanation: In spam detection, precision asks 'Of emails marked spam, how many are actually spam?' while recall asks 'Of actual spam emails, how many did we find?' High precision means few false positives; high recall means few false negatives. Different tasks prioritize differently.

questions.q: What is F1 score, and in what situations is it preferred over accuracy for NLP tasks?
options: F1 score is the average of true positives and true negatives
F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall; it's preferred when classes are imbalanced and accuracy is misleading
F1 score only applies to regression tasks
F1 score is outdated and never used in modern NLP
questions.explanation: With 99% spam and 1% ham, a model predicting everything as spam gets 99% accuracy but catches no ham. F1 score balances precision and recall, giving a better picture of performance on imbalanced datasets. It's standard for NLP classification tasks.

questions.q: What is BLEU score and why is it the standard automatic metric for machine translation evaluation?
options: BLEU measures word error rate by counting misspellings in translations
BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) compares machine-generated translations to reference translations using n-gram overlap, with scores from 0 to 100
BLEU requires human evaluation
BLEU cannot handle multiple reference translations
questions.explanation: BLEU computes the fraction of n-grams in the system output that appear in reference translations, using a brevity penalty to discourage very short outputs. It correlates reasonably with human judgment and enables automatic comparison of translation systems, though it has limitations with paraphrasing and synonyms.

questions.q: What does ROUGE score measure, and how does it differ from BLEU?
options: ROUGE and BLEU measure identical concepts
BLEU emphasizes precision (what the system generates matches references), while ROUGE emphasizes recall (what's in the reference is captured by the system); ROUGE is preferred for summarization
ROUGE is only for translation and BLEU is only for summarization
Both metrics completely ignore word order
questions.explanation: BLEU is precision-oriented (n-grams in output that match), while ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) measures n-gram recall. For summarization, catching important reference content (recall) matters more than avoiding additions, making ROUGE more appropriate.

questions.q: What is perplexity as an evaluation metric for language models?
options: Perplexity measures how confused humans are by model output
Perplexity measures the average negative log-probability assigned to held-out test data; lower perplexity indicates the model assigns higher probability to correct text
Perplexity cannot be computed for neural language models
Perplexity values above 1000 are always good
questions.explanation: Perplexity = 2^(cross-entropy) represents the effective branching factor. A language model with perplexity 50 essentially thinks 50 words are equally likely at each position. Lower perplexity means better predictions on the test set.

questions.q: In the context of Named Entity Recognition, what does the F1 score at the token level (vs. entity level) measure differently?
options: They measure identical things with different names
Token-level F1 evaluates per-token predictions; entity-level F1 requires entire entities to be correctly identified (all tokens + correct boundaries), which is stricter and typically lower
Only token-level F1 matters for NER
Entity-level F1 is impossible to compute
questions.explanation: An NER system might correctly tag most tokens of 'New York City' but mislabel 'City', failing at token level. Even getting all tokens right but with wrong boundaries (e.g., 'New York [PERSON]' instead of '[LOCATION]') fails entity-level evaluation. Entity-level is more realistic.

questions.q: What is accuracy as an evaluation metric, and when is it misleading for NLP tasks?
options: Accuracy measures the proportion of correct predictions regardless of class distribution; it's misleading for imbalanced datasets where a trivial baseline can achieve high accuracy
Accuracy only applies to regression tasks
Accuracy is always the best metric for NLP
Accuracy cannot be computed for multi-class problems
questions.explanation: With 95% negative examples, predicting all negatives achieves 95% accuracy but is useless. Precision, recall, and F1 provide more nuanced evaluation. Accuracy is reliable only with balanced datasets.

questions.q: What is the Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and what NLP task is it particularly suited for?
options: MRR measures word frequency in documents
MRR computes 1 divided by the rank of the first correct result, useful for information retrieval and question answering where finding any correct answer is important and earlier answers are better
MRR only works for classification tasks
MRR requires all answers to be ranked before computation
questions.explanation: In question answering, if correct answers appear at ranks 2, 5, 8, the MRR is (1/2 + 1/5 + 1/8) / 3 ≈ 0.34. MRR rewards finding correct answers early, making it ideal for ranking tasks where position matters more than covering all possibilities.

questions.q: What is the relationship between cross-entropy loss (training objective) and perplexity (evaluation metric) for language models?
options: Cross-entropy and perplexity are unrelated metrics
Perplexity is 2 raised to the power of cross-entropy; optimizing cross-entropy during training directly minimizes perplexity
Cross-entropy is only for classification and perplexity is only for regression
Both metrics ignore probability distributions
questions.explanation: A model trained to minimize cross-entropy loss automatically minimizes perplexity, since perplexity = 2^(cross-entropy). This direct relationship makes cross-entropy the standard training objective for language models.

questions.q: Why do metrics like BLEU and ROUGE sometimes give high scores to translations/summaries that humans find poor?
options: Humans always make mistakes in evaluation while automatic metrics are perfect
These metrics compute n-gram overlap and don't account for synonymous expressions, paraphrasing, or semantic equivalence, so a grammatically wrong but textually similar output scores high
Automatic metrics are biased toward short outputs
BLEU and ROUGE are never used in practice
questions.explanation: A translation 'The cat is sitting' might score 0 on BLEU compared to reference 'The feline is resting' despite being semantically equivalent. Modern evaluation increasingly uses learned metrics like BERTScore or human evaluation to capture semantic similarity better than n-gram overlap.
